text: The surgeon transects the gastrocolic ligament, followed by taking down the hepatic flexure of the colon. The V-junction is identified in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the duodenal jejunal fold and secures the proximal jejunum mucosa using a bipolar device.
The surgeon moves to the right side of the patient and identifies the staphylococcal ligament, which is then transected.

The surgeon identifies and dissects lymph node group VI along the uncinate mesenteric group at the lateral border of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). With effective traction and counter-traction, the dissection plane is clearly visible. The lymph nodes are clipped and secured using a bipolar device.
The surgeon identifies the gastrocolic trunk of Henle and its tributaries, clipping and sealing the gastroepiploic vein using a bipolar device.
The surgeon dissects the unseen end of the mesentery group, preparing for the separation and transfer process.

The surgeon pulls up the duodenum and performs full coagulation. The duodenum is retracted to the right, allowing for the transaction on the right side. A small inferior pancreaticoduodenal branch (IPDB) is sealed using a ligature.
The surgeon retracts the thaumat downward and transects the laser omentum. A Natanza retractor is applied to elevate the liver, providing better access to the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the Thaumat at the antrum using an endostapler. Lymph node group VIII is dissected along the upper border of the pancreas.
The assistant surgeon retracts the pancreas downward while using a dissector in the left hand and an Adaswitch device to dissect around the lymph node.
The surgeon secures the left clastic vein using a surgical clip and the Vibora device.
The surgeon dissects lymph node groups VIII and XIp away from the common hepatic artery and stropionic artery. The ligastic vein is skipped and divided during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a silver finger instrument to tunnel the space between the portal vein and the pancreatic neck, then encircles the pancreatic neck with hernia tape.
The surgeon stands between the patient's legs to perform HILA dissection, focusing on the lymph nodes along the pepto-adrenal ligament, which are dissected and moved to the right side.
The surgeon secures the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) using hemoclips, then identifies and divides the artery with precision.
The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy, encircling the common pancreatic duct (CPD) with a seal to prevent spillage from the tumor site. The CPD is then ligated and divided using endocysts.
The surgeon uses an ataxotic device to divide the ankylotic polynema. The pancreatic duct, located at the posterior inferior side of the pancreatic parenchyma, is cut using an endosister.
The surgeon clips and secures the Plexus admisor pancreas using a bipolar device. The PSPDB is identified and similarly clipped and secured with the bipolar device.
The surgeon identifies the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery (IPDA), clips it, and seals it using a bipolar device. Subsequently, the POSIMOM-TETRENUM is divided using an endostepper instrument.
The surgeon removes the supersamine using a plastic bag, ensuring containment and preventing spillage during extraction.
